coredata.dc:description: Due to the serious detrimental impact on human health, antibiotic pollution particularly tetracyclines residues has become a serious problem. Herein, a multiple response fluorescent probe consisted of dual-emission carbon dots and Eu3+ (D-CDs@Eu3+) is designed for the determination and discrimination of tetracyclines (TCs). Specifically, the carboxyl and amidogen group of dual-emission carbon dots (D-CDs) can coordinate with Eu3+ to form the D-CDs@Eu3+. Upon adding TCs, the fluorescence intensities of D-CDs at 405 nm and 495 nm are quenched due to inner filter effect (IFE) and the localization of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (L-FRET) between the D-CDs@Eu3+ and TC. Simultaneously, the D-CDs@Eu3+ may chelate with TCs to enhance the occurrence of antenna effect, while the characteristic peaks of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 615 nm are enhanced. On these bases, the TCs detection is achieved with low detection limits from 46.7 to 72.0 nM. Additionally, through the distinct efficiencies of L-FRET, the discrimination of TCs is achieved. Moreover, a novel centrifugated lateral flow assay strips (CLFASs) device is developed by integrating the D-CDs@Eu3+, lateral flow assay strips and smartphone using RGB variations for TCs detection, achieving remarkable recoveries (98.6–103.7 %) in real samples. Therefore, this CLFASs device provides a reliable approach for the TCs detection, demonstrating potential applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the serious detrimental impact on human health, antibiotic pollution particularly tetracyclines residues has become a serious problem. Herein, a multiple response fluorescent probe consisted of dual-emission carbon dots and Eu3+ (D-CDs@Eu3+) is designed for the determination and discrimination of tetracyclines (TCs). Specifically, the carboxyl and amidogen group of dual-emission carbon dots (D-CDs) can coordinate with Eu3+ to form the D-CDs@Eu3+. Upon adding TCs, the fluorescence intensities of D-CDs at 405 nm and 495 nm are quenched due to inner filter effect (IFE) and the localization of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (L-FRET) between the D-CDs@Eu3+ and TC. Simultaneously, the D-CDs@Eu3+ may chelate with TCs to enhance the occurrence of antenna effect, while the characteristic peaks of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 615 nm are enhanced. On these bases, the TCs detection is achieved with low detection limits from 46.7 to 72.0 nM. Additionally, through the distinct efficiencies of L-FRET, the discrimination of TCs is achieved. Moreover, a novel centrifugated lateral flow assay strips (CLFASs) device is developed by integrating the D-CDs@Eu3+, lateral flow assay strips and smartphone using RGB variations for TCs detection, achieving remarkable recoveries (98.6–103.7 %) in real samples. Therefore, this CLFASs device provides a reliable approach for the TCs detection, demonstrating potential applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the introduction of antibiotics, over 10,000 tons have been used annually as drugs and food additives in environment, food production and pharmaceuticals. Therein, tetracyclines (TCs) represent a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics. The discharge of TCs into the environment has led to the development of resistance among disease-causing microorganisms and the threat to the living organisms and ecosystems health. In this study, a multiple response fluorescent probe is designed for the selective determination and discrimination with preferable low detection limits. Moreover, a novel centrifugated lateral flow assay strips device is developed for TCs detection achieving remarkable recoveries in real samples.

Since the introduction of antibiotics, over 10,000 tons have been used annually as drugs and food additives in environment, food production and pharmaceuticals (Li et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2019). Among these, tetracyclines (TCs) including tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DOX), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC), represent a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics with antibacterial activity (Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b). However, the discharge of TCs into the environment has led to the development of resistance among disease-causing microorganisms, the transmission of antibiotic resistance genes and the threat to the living organisms and ecosystems health (Wu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019a, 2019b). Moreover, it has been claimed that the persistent and recurrent consumption of TCs is associated with adverse side effects, including hepatotoxicity, gastrointestinal disturbance and anaphylactic reaction (Nabavi et al., 2022). The deaths numbers due to the abuse of TCs annually escalate to 10 million, as the World Health Organization reported (Giersing et al., 2019). Besides, the presence of diverse TCs in environmental mixtures poses significant challenges for eco-environmental restoration due to the inherent difficulties in species discrimination (Li et al., 2023a, 2023b). Therefore, developing an efficient, accurate and convenient method for determination and discrimination of TCs is of vital significance in the environment.

Conventional methods commonly used for the TCs detection include capillary electrophoresis (Bosma et al., 2020), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Le et al., 2012), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Pang et al., 2021) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Qi et al., 2024). Unfortunately, most of these methods often necessitate expensive and large instruments, extensive preparation steps and skilled personnel, which are the inability to carry out on-site portability (Han et al., 2020). Due to their extensive sources, low toxicity, superior photoluminescence and biocompatibility, the fluorescent methods based on carbon dots (CDs) garner significant attention (Wang et al., 2022a, 2022b; Li et al., 2023a, 2023b). There are abundances of fluorescence probes proposed for the detection of TCs using the modulation of fluorescent intensity between CDs and target molecules. For example, Zhang et al. used cerium‑nitrogen co-doped CDs to detect TCs via the single emission changes at 440 nm (Zhang and Fan, 2021). Xing et al. developed S, N-doped CDs for detection of TCs utilizing fluorescence quenching due to the inner filter effect (IFE) (Xing et al., 2020). However, these fluorescent probes with a single signal output are susceptible to environmental interference and background noise (Guo et al., 2023a; Hu et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2022). To address these limitations, the ratiometric fluorescent probes are highly desirable which have multiple different the fluorescent signals. These probes employ the ratio of two output signals instead of the absolute value of a single signal as the detected signal to provide a function of built-in self-calibration and improve the anti-interference ability, robustness and reliability of probe (Qiu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019a, 2019b). Additionally, it is essential to create a fluorescence probe with multiple signals for TCs residue detection. In recent years, lanthanides with the unique chemical, physical and photophysical properties have garnered significant interest in the advancement of fluorescent materials owing to their significant Stokes shift, sharp linear emission band and extended fluorescence lifetimes (Huang et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017). Among them, the β-diketone structure in TCs chelates with europium ion (Eu3+) spontaneously to form the Eu3+-TCs complex with bright fluorescence, and the absorbed energy from ligand is rapidly transferred to Eu3+ through the antenna effect (AE), resulting in enhancing the red fluorescence emissions of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 615 nm (DeOliveira et al., 2007). For instances, Yang et al. constructed a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for TC detection based on a CDs and Eu3+ system (Yang et al., 2020). However, due to the complex synthesis process, unknown cytotoxicity and unknown material stability limit their application in TC detection. On the basis, the development of Eu3+-functionalized CDs-based sensing platform is expected to be a highly accurate and innovative strategy.

Moreover, it is known that making assessments with naked eye inevitably possesses the risk of personal error. Smartphone provides a wonderful idea for excluding the individual difference to improve the accuracy of visual analysis. Nevertheless, to our knowledge, only a few miniature devices based on smartphone are explored for the identification and differentiation of TCs (Zhu et al., 2023). Simultaneously, the flow assay strips with no sophisticated equipment, simple fabrication process and low cost offer the potential for rapid and convenient testing in the recent years (He et al., 2021). Various types of the flow assay strip, such as vertical flow strips and lateral flow strips, are still passively driven by capillary force and lack actively adjustable change (Mäntylä et al., 2003; Yee et al., 2017). On this basis, the centrifugal lateral flow assay strips (CLFASs) enhance the effectiveness of the flow assay strips through integrating the them into centrifugal microfluidic platforms with active flow control (Dashtian et al., 2023). More importantly, CLFASs combined with the image processing capability of smartphone devices can realize the automatic recognition and extraction, showing the benefits of portability, intelligence and rapidity (Zhu et al., 2022). Consequently, the development of a visual real-time quantitative device based on CLFASs and smartphone for TCs determination and discrimination in the environmental monitoring is a key point of the current research.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence probe (D-CDs@Eu3+) is established using dual-emission carbon dots (D-CDs) and Eu3+ for the real-time determination and discrimination of TCs in environmental samples (Scheme 1 ). To be specific, upon the addition of TCs, the two emission peaks (405 nm and 495 nm) of D-CDs decrease through the localization of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (L-FRET) and IFE. Meanwhile, the emissions peaks at 590 nm and 615 nm from the characteristic peaks of Eu3+ are obviously activated due to the AE. On this basis, by selecting the radio of different emission peaks, compared with other methods, the proposed fluorescence probe shows that low limits of detection (LODs) for TC, DOX, OTC and CTC are 46.7 nM, 54.3 nM, 72.0 nM and 57.9 nM, respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence response patterns of single and mixed TCs solution are analyzed using linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) to effectively distinguish TCs with high precision. Furthermore, a novel centrifugal device which integrates the CLFASs, D-CDs@Eu3+ and smartphone utilizes the chromogenic change for determination and discrimination of TCs, achieving excellent LODs of 91.4 nM, 98.4 nM, 123.2 nM and 105.7 nM, with admirable recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD). Moreover, the device utilizes the centrifugal force to control the liquid to flow through the strips, which demonstrate satisfactory results in real sample detection. Thus, our works provide a new choice and idea for the design of a new type of multifunctional fluorescent sensor and lateral flow assay strips.

The detailed description of reagents, instruments, synthesis of the D-CDs@Eu3+, fluorescence determination and discrimination of TCs, fabrication of the CLFASs device and analysis of the CLFASs device were shown in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of the D-CDs@Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: The characterizations of D-CDs and D-CDs@Eu3+ are determined through a series of analyses, such as the morphology, chemical and elemental (Fig. S1-S4). Meanwhile, the measurement about optical characteristics of the D-CDs is conducted (Fig. S5-S6). These pertinent findings and discussions are shown in the Supporting Information. As depicted in Fig. 1(a), the introduction of Eu3+ shows minimal impact on the emission peak intensity of D-CDs. Meanwhile, the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ are deactivated, caused by the occupation of unsaturated ligand sites by unbound water molecules (Liu et al., 2024a, 2024b). For a more in-depth investigation into the fluorescence properties of D-CDs@Eu3+, the D-CDs@Eu3+ exhibit three apparent peaks at 226 nm for π-π* transition correspond to the CC and at 280 nm and 354 nm for n-π* transition attributed to the CO bond (Fig. 1(b)) (Li et al., 2023a, 2023b; Tang et al., 2022). Under the excitation peaks in 356 nm, D-CDs@Eu3+ demonstrate dual emission peaks at 405 nm and 495 nm, as same as the fluorescence properties of D-CDs. As portrayed in Fig. 1(c), fluorescence intensity of D-CDs@Eu3+ remains stable for up to 22 h of UV irradiation, indicating high resistance to photobleaching. Besides, negligible changes in fluorescence intensity are observed after being stored at room temperature for 33 days (Fig. 1(d)), signifying the prominent durability of the synthesized D-CDs@Eu3+. To sum up, the exceptional optical stability of D-CDs@Eu3+ ensures reliable and accurate sensing capability in real inspection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection and selectivity of D-CDs@Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para:
To enhance the detection efficacy, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE) is evaluated to optimize the detection conditions including dosage, temperature and time (Ma et al., 2023), which are derived from Eq. (1):
(1)
QE =
I 615
/
I 405
−
I 615
/
I 405
0
/
I 615
/
I 405
0
where (I 405 /I 615 ) 0 and I 405 /I 615 are the radio of fluorescence intensities of D-CDs@Eu3+ without and with TCs, respectively. Under different dosages of D-CDs@Eu3+ (from 5 to 35 μL), the QE of D-CDs@Eu3+ in the presence of TCs initially increases and then decreases (Fig. S7(a)), showing that the optimal dosage of D-CDs@Eu3+ is 25 μL. For the optimal temperature, the QE is observed at the temperature (5 °C–65 °C) and shows the maximum value at 25 °C (Fig. S7(b)). The QE of the D-CDs@Eu3+ exhibits a significant increase in the initial incubation of 2 min and subsequently attains stability, as shown in Fig. S7(c). Subsequently, the reaction time is established as 2 min. Under the optimized experimental conditions, TC as the representative analyte is chosen to elucidate the sensing principle and detection process of TCs. Interestingly, dual emission peaks of D-CDs at 405 nm and 495 nm gradually decrease (Fig. 2(a)). At the same time, the intensities of two peaks at 590 nm and 615 nm demonstrate a gradual increase, which are ascribed to the sensitized characteristic red fluorescent emissions of Eu3+. The fluorescence color exhibits a gradually transition from cyan to red after adding TC in the solutions (Fig. 2(b)). On this basis, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 615/I 405 increases with the TC concentration ranging from 0 to 300 μM, as demonstrated in Fig. 2(c). The equation of linear regression is described as Y = 0.0146×-0.0259 (R2 = 0.9807) with calculated LOD of 46.7 nM using threefold standard deviation of blank measurements to slope of calibration (Fig. 2(d)). Correspondingly, D-CDs@Eu3+ also exhibit good linear relationships with LODs of 54.3 nM, 72.0 nM and 57.9 nM for DOX, OTC and CTC, respectively (Fig. S8). Compared with the reported results, the obtained D-CDs@Eu3+ exhibit relatively low LODs and a wide linear range for detecting TCs (Table S1).

The selectivity of D-CDs@Eu3+ is examined against a wide range of potential interfering substances, including similar compounds, cations and anions (Gly, Arg, Lys, Cys, Trp, ENR, CIP, PEF, FLE, MAR, ENO, CHL, ERY, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, I−, Br−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, ClO4 −). As depicted in Fig. S9(a), only TCs increase the I 615/I 405 ratio, while other substances generated inconsiderable effect. Additionally, as observed in Fig. S9(b), the presence of TCs leads to a significant increasement in the I 615/I 405 of D-CDs@Eu3+, indicating the excellent anti-interference of probe for the TCs detection. The results indicate that the proposed sensor possesses preeminent selectively in determining TCs.

To demonstrate the reaction mechanism of D-CDs@Eu3+ toward TCs, a series of experimental techniques are carried out using TC as an instance. On the one hand, the addition of TC leads to a decrease in the fluorescence intensities of D-CDs@Eu3+ at 405 nm and 495 nm. Interestingly, both the emission and excitation spectra of the D-CDs@Eu3+ partial overlap with the absorption spectrum of TC (Fig. 2(e)), showing that the fluorescent sensing of D-CDs@Eu3+ toward TC is caused by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE (Revesz et al., 2022; Sai et al., 2020). Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetimes of D-CDs@Eu3+ are investigated in the absence and presence of TC. The fluorescence lifetimes of D-CDs@Eu3+ at 495 nm remain nearly constant with values of 2.632 ns and 2.404 ns (before and after the introduction of TC), respectively, as depicted in Fig. 2(f). This tiny alteration indicates that the IFE presents as a quenching mechanism, while FRET may play a negligible role in the fluorescence quenching at 495 nm (Zu et al., 2017). Additionally, the fluorescence decay lifetime of D-CDs@Eu3+ (405 nm) is 1.274 ns, whereas it exhibits shorter (0.813 ns) after TC adding (Fig. 2(g)), indicating that except for the IFE, FRET is also implicated in the process of fluorescence quenching (Li et al., 2024; Yan et al., 2019). Concurrently, Fig. S10 shows nearly similar the measurement findings of other TCs. Through the different forms of FRET observed in the two peaks, we deduce that quenching mechanisms are IFE and L-FRET, which lead to different degrees of observed quenching within each peak. To better confirm the existence of IFE, the derived quench efficiencies show that the quenching efficiencies from IFE are 27.9 % and 31.6 % (Fig. S11), corresponding to the florescence peaks at 405 nm and 495 nm, which confirms the existence of the IFE mechanism and the other mechanism (Wang et al., 2015). As we known, the efficiency of energy transfer between the donor and the quencher is influenced by the degree of effective spectral overlap (Guo et al., 2023b; Li et al., 2021; Mahto et al., 2023). To further verify our inference, the efficiencies of FRET are determined based on the average fluorescence lifetimes following Eq. (2) (Venugopalan and Vidya, 2023):
(2)
E = 1 −
τ DA
τ D
where E represents the FRET efficiency, whereas τ DA and τ D are the fluorescence lifetimes values of D-CDs@Eu3+ with and without TC, respectively. Table S4 demonstrates that E 405 and E 495 are 36.18 % and 8.66 %, which illustrate that different FRET efficiency exists in two emission peaks of D-CDs@Eu3+, thereby confirming our initial inference (Wang et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2021). Furthermore, the calculated results of other TCs are nearly similar, indicating consistent quenching mechanism within the fluorescence quenching processes.

On the other hands, the fluorescence emission peaks at 590 nm and 615 nm are obviously enhanced. The addition of TC alone to the Eu3+ demonstrates an inconspicuous enhancement, yielding as disparate results as those obtained with D-CDs@Eu3+, as presented in Fig. S12. We assume that D-CDs may serve as an additional ligand in place of water molecules, thereby amplifying the fluorescence intensity between TC and Eu3+ (Tan and Chen, 2012). Likewise, the absorption peaks of the D-CDs@Eu3+ exhibit a red shift (360 nm–397 nm) after the addition of TC, which indicate that the D-CDs@Eu3+ may chelate with TC (Fig. 2(h)) (Zhang et al., 2021). As depicted in Fig. S13, a slight shift is observed in the C=O/C=N and OH of D-CDs@Eu3+ with TC, indicating that the oxygen atom in carbonyl group of TC can form a chelation complex with Eu3+ (Fan et al., 2023). In addition, the content of C=O/C=N from D-CDs@Eu3+ with TC are decreased compared to the initial spectra of D-CDs@Eu3+ (Fig. S14) (Zhang et al., 2023). Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. S15, the D-CDs show a negative charge, whereas the zeta potentials of the D-CDs@Eu3+ experience an increase from 4.7 mV to 13.5 mV upon the addition of TC owing to the presence of the hydroxyl groups in TC, which have ability to counteract the negative charge on the surface of D-CDs (Liu et al., 2021). Based on these findings, the chelation and electrostatic interaction facilitate a formation of the interaction strength between D-CDs@Eu3+ and TC. Using the Benesi-Hildebrand (Yang et al., 2022), the binding constant (K BH) is calculated as 7.022 × 104 M−1 for TC, which is similarity with the interaction strengths of DOX, OTC and CTC (Fig. S16). As we all known, TC can chelate with Eu3+ and transfer energy to Eu3+ though AE, which possesses a β-diketone structure exhibiting a significantly molar extinction coefficient (Fig. S17) (Fu et al., 2022). Thus, the electrostatic interaction reduces the distance between D-CDs@Eu3+ and TC, allowing TC to successfully chelate with Eu3+ from D-CDs@Eu3+, thereby promoting the energy transfer (Yao et al., 2022). Moreover, as included in Fig. S18, the triplet and singlet state energy level of TC are 19,160 cm−1 and 36,200 cm−1, respectively. The energy level of Eu3+ (5D0, 17,300 cm−1) is about 1,860 cm−1 lower than the triplet state of TC, while the calculated energy gap between the triplet and singlet state energy levels of TC is 17,040 cm−1, in which intersystem crossing process may occur. On these bases, TC, received energy from D-CDs though IFE and L-FRET, leads to its energy transition from its ground state to an excited state. Subsequently, the part of this energy is transferred to the triplet excited state through intersystem crossing (Kong et al., 2007; Wu et al., 2021). In the excited state of the triplet, a portion of the energy is transferred to excite the Eu3+, inducing characteristic fluorescence emission (5D0 → 7F1 and 5D0 → 7F2), which suggest that TC can act as an ‘antenna’ to effectively sensitize the fluorescence intensities at 590 nm and 615 nm of Eu3+ (He et al., 2022). In summary, TC quenches the fluorescence peaks of D-CDs@Eu3+ at 405 nm and 495 nm though the synergistic effect of IFE and L-FRET, causing distinct quenching at these two peaks. Meanwhile, the synergistic cooperation between TC (as the antenna ligand) and D-CDs (as the ancillary ligand) exerts the AE and transfers the energy to Eu3+, which results in the activation of Eu3+ emissions at 590 nm and 615 nm. The schematic representation of D-CDs@Eu3+ to TC is presented in Fig. 2(i). Notably, the uneven overlap of TCs absorption spectra and D-CDs@Eu3+ emission, which show four different signal channel responses (405 nm, 495 nm, 590 nm and 615 nm), conduces a fundamental basis for discrimination.

The four signal channel responses of D-CDs@Eu3+ to each TCs are recorded (F1-F4) and the relative signal change in fluorescence responses is quantified by (I
I 0)/I 0, which I 0 and I are the fluorescent emission intensities without and with analytes, respectively (Liu et al., 2024a, 2024b). As shown in Fig. 3(a), diverse recognition patterns can be formed by collecting the response signals from the D-CDs@Eu3+ sensor. To clearly demonstrate the response patterns for each analyte, we transform the response pattern to a heat map (Fig. 3(b)), confirming this sensing strategy can intuitively discriminate different TCs. The developed LDA study of D-CDs@Eu3+ is shown in Fig. 3(c). This 3D LDA score plot selects the three primary variables that account for all of the variance contributed by canonical factors. The 20-output data (4 TCs × 5 replicates) are distinctly designated as five separate clusters without cross impacts. For more clarity, as illustrated in Fig. S19, using the HCA classification method, every TCs are divided into the same subset with no misclassification, achieving a remarkable accuracy rate of 100 %. As illustrated in Fig. 3(d-f), the three concentrations of TCs (25 μM, 50 μM and 75 μM) are correctly classified into four separate clusters, respectively, without any instances of misclassification or overlap among the clusters. Meanwhile, in Fig. S20, the HCA results also demonstrate that each of the 20 cases are accurately categorized into its respective category. What's more, each curve starts at the exact same concentration (0 μM), as evident in 3D LDA score plots (Fig. 3(g)). The LDA based response plot clearly demonstrates the ability of D-CDs@Eu3+ to discriminate TCs at the different concentration, revealing the progressive spatial changes exhibited by each TCs as the concentration increases. Furthermore, for validating the practical reliability of TCs discrimination, the fluorescent responses toward the binary mixtures containing TC and DOX (the total TCs concentration is 100 μM) are gauged by the D-CDs@Eu3+ sensor. As depicted in Fig. 3(h-i), the findings demonstrate that the pure TC, DOX and even their mixtures can be distinguished in the LDA and the HCA analyses. Notably, the results affirm that each of the 35 cases are accurately categorized into its appropriate category. Thus, the feasibility and accuracy of our proposed method for distinguishing TCs are confirmed by the LDA and HCA data, including more intricate cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and detection principle of visual CLFASs device
ce:section.ce:para: In order to provide an affordable and portable solution for detecting the multiple TCs, a simple and robust device based CLFASs is developed. This device enables analysis of up to three samples for the determination and discrimination of TCs through parallel computation. To begin with, the components of CLFASs are designed by AutoCAD and identified by 3D printer, while the remaining parts along with the pallet are crafted form PMMA sheets using a computer numerical control. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the CLFASs consist of two layers and the top layer has three detection areas with loading chamber, access chamber, reaction chamber and waste chamber. Among them, the D-CDs@Eu3+ are incorporated into NC membrane to create a T-line for on-site sensing with the self-calibration capability. Subsequently, the NC membrane is cut into a designed pattern using a cutting plotter, which is embedded in CLFASs (Fig. 4(b)). Moreover, the bottom layer is a 3 mm thick PMMA, which is connected to the pallet. After that, as illustrated in Fig. 4(c), the diagrammatic depiction of the device based CLFASs is presented. Besides, the pallet is connected with the CLFASs, while the stepper motor is connected under the pallet, resulting in the CLFASs rotated through the work of the motor. Fig. S21 shows the physical photo.

Subsequently, the detection procedure using D-CDs@Eu3+ for the determination and discrimination of TCs is illustrated in Fig. 4(d). After the sample is injected into the loading chamber through access window, the motor positioned beneath the setup drives the pallet, enabling it to rotate the CLFASs at a predetermined speed. The liquid on the left of the access chamber flows through the T-line and C-line before ultimately reaching the waste chamber due to centrifugal and capillary forces. Thus, quantitative analysis is performed with a calibration curve prepared by plotting the RGB values from T-line against TCs concentration (Cai et al., 2019; Shu et al., 2018). It is noted that the quantity of detection samples can be flexibly modified based on specific requirements, with the capability to test up to 3 samples simultaneously.

To achieve the best analytical performance of the experimental system, some crucial conditions including rotation speed, the D-CDs@Eu3+ concentration and response time are optimized. Specifically, the R/G value of T-line initially rises and then drops with the rotation speed adding (800–1500 rpm), indicating that the optimal rotation speed at 1200 rpm (Fig. S22(a)). Moreover, as shown in Fig. S22(b), it is noticed that the value of R/G reaches a maximum at a concentration of 1.0 mg∙mL−1, which is selected as the optimum concentration of N-CDs@Eu3+. Furthermore, to explore the best response time of the CLFASs device after the addition of TCs, the change of R/G of the nano-sensor after the addition of TCs is recorded in a certain time interval, showing that 8 min is the optimum response time (Fig. S22(c)). Therefore, for achieving the precision and consistent detection results, the optimal conditions of CLFASs sensing device are 1.0 mg∙mL−1 of N-CDs@Eu3+ for 8 min at 1200 rpm.

Based on the optimal conditions, the TC is selected as the analytical model and formulated with different concentrations (ranging from 0 to 300 μM) through testing experiments. The overall detection process involves the solution addition, CLFASs operation and RGB analysis (Fig. 5(a)). Obviously, the solution is injected into the loading chamber and flows though the T-line and C-line, ultimately reaching the waste chamber as depicted in Video S1. Moreover, the validity of the data is demonstrated through the color change from colorless to red in the C-line. Fig. 5(b) depicts photos of CLFASs device with different concentration of TC under UV light, which exhibits that T-line clearly changes from cyan to red. Furtherly, as illustrated in Fig. 5(c-d), the RGB values of T-line are collected through the APP on the smartphone, showing a good linear correlation with TC concentration (R/G = 0.00762CTC + 0.42881) and achieve a LOD of 91.4 nM. Meanwhile, the LODs of DOX, OTC and CTC in their respective ranges are calculated to be 98.4 nM, 123.2 nM and 105.7 nM by similar monitoring steps (Fig. S23). Moreover, to assess the practicability of the method, we study the detection effect of TC by the CLFASs device in real samples, and the fluorescence images of T-line are obtained in Fig. 5(e). The recovery rate (98.6–103.7 %) and the RSD (1.9–3.5 %) are satisfactory, which indicate that the developed device can be used for detection of TCs in actual samples (Fig. 5(f)). Additionally, HPLC is used as the standard method for TC detection to verify the accuracy of the above device. The recovery is in the range of 96.8–102.8 %, and the RSD is in the range of 1.5–3.6 % (Table S5). According to the Student's t-test method, we could observe that all t values in these cases were below 2.78 at the 0.05 significance level (t crit[0.05,4] = 2.78), thereby exhibiting well-matched accuracy between two methods (Zeng et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2019). The t-test results (p < 0.05) show that there are no significant disparities between these methods. Thus, CLFASs device exhibits remarkable specificity toward TCs and validates its excellent potential for detecting and discriminating various TCs species.

Based on the optimal conditions, the TC is selected as the analytical model and formulated with different concentrations (ranging from 0 to 300 μM) through testing experiments. The overall detection process involves the solution addition, CLFASs operation and RGB analysis (Fig. 5(a)). Obviously, the solution is injected into the loading chamber and flows though the T-line and C-line, ultimately reaching the waste chamber as depicted in Video S1. Moreover, the validity of the data is demonstrated through the color change from colorless to red in the C-line. Fig. 5(b) depicts photos of CLFASs device with different concentration of TC under UV light, which exhibits that T-line clearly changes from cyan to red. Furtherly, as illustrated in Fig. 5(c-d), the RGB values of T-line are collected through the APP on the smartphone, showing a good linear correlation with TC concentration (R/G = 0.00762CTC + 0.42881) and achieve a LOD of 91.4 nM. Meanwhile, the LODs of DOX, OTC and CTC in their respective ranges are calculated to be 98.4 nM, 123.2 nM and 105.7 nM by similar monitoring steps (Fig. S23). Moreover, to assess the practicability of the method, we study the detection effect of TC by the CLFASs device in real samples, and the fluorescence images of T-line are obtained in Fig. 5(e). The recovery rate (98.6–103.7 %) and the RSD (1.9–3.5 %) are satisfactory, which indicate that the developed device can be used for detection of TCs in actual samples (Fig. 5(f)). Additionally, HPLC is used as the standard method for TC detection to verify the accuracy of the above device. The recovery is in the range of 96.8–102.8 %, and the RSD is in the range of 1.5–3.6 % (Table S5). According to the Student's t-test method, we could observe that all t values in these cases were below 2.78 at the 0.05 significance level (t crit[0.05,4] = 2.78), thereby exhibiting well-matched accuracy between two methods (Zeng et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2019). The t-test results (p < 0.05) show that there are no significant disparities between these methods. Thus, CLFASs device exhibits remarkable specificity toward TCs and validates its excellent potential for detecting and discriminating various TCs species.

In summary, the fluorescence probe based on D-CDs@Eu3+ shows a strong linear correlation with different TC concentrations over the scope of 0–100 μM with the LOD of 46.7 nM. Likewise, the LODs of DOX, OTC and CTC are 54.3 nM, 72.0 nM and 57.9 nM, respectively. Furthermore, when treated with LDA and HCA, the identification and determination of four TCs are achieved with 100 % accuracy. In addition, the novel CLFASs device with D-CDs@Eu3+ is designed to address the challenges in real-time determination and discrimination of TCs in environmental samples. Compared with conventional methods, several advantages of this device can be highlighted as follows: (i) CLFASs combined with smartphone devices can realize sample detection due to centrifugal and capillary forces, showing the benefits of intelligence and rapidity; (ii) using multiple sampling instead of the single extracting could effectively eliminate the influences of background fluctuation, thus improving its anti-interference ability; and (iii) the CLFASs device is characterized by its simplicity, obviating the need for costly instrumentation and specialized personnel. Nevertheless, our sensing strategy has certain limitations in terms of integration and assay process complexity. Therefore, future endeavors should prioritize the development and implementation of a miniaturized and highly integrated detection device.
